does: This paper introduces GETA, a generative evolving testing approach designed to dynamically assess value alignment in large language models without suffering from benchmark saturation. Rather than relying on static test sets that may leak into training data or become outdated, GETA generates test items on-the-fly that are tailored to each model's specific capabilities and moral boundaries. The method learns a joint distribution of item difficulty and model value conformity, allowing the evaluation framework to co-evolve alongside rapidly improving LLMs. The approach is grounded in adaptive testing methodologies from measurement theory and directly addresses the evaluation chrono-effect problem where existing benchmarks become less reliable as models advance.
connects: This work extends a long tradition of measuring bias, toxicity, and ethical alignment in language models through the lens of adaptive testing theory. It builds on prior work in value alignment benchmarking such as social bias detection frameworks and ethical evaluation suites, while responding specifically to limitations identified in static benchmark designs. The paper connects to adaptive testing methods developed in psychometrics and educational measurement, applying these established techniques to the novel challenge of continuously evolving AI systems. It also engages with broader concerns about benchmark saturation and data contamination in rapid AI development cycles, issues increasingly recognized across the evaluation community.
enables: This approach opens pathways for developing continuously updated evaluation frameworks that remain relevant as LLMs evolve. By enabling dynamic test generation rather than reliance on fixed benchmarks, GETA could support more reliable regulatory assessment and certification of AI systems throughout their lifecycle. The method potentially enables better detection of value misalignment even as models become more sophisticated or develop new failure modes. Future work could extend this framework to other safety dimensions beyond ethics, apply it to multimodal systems, or develop meta-level understanding of how models' moral boundaries shift over time and with scale, informing both development practices and governance approaches.
